node_2: semantic image editing by disentangling object and background
edge: SIEDOB is the novel paradigm proposed to disentangle objects and backgrounds in image editing.
SIEDOB uses subnetworks for objects and backgrounds to disassemble edited input into background regions and objects.

SIEDOB proposes innovative designs to produce high-quality edited images, including Semantic-Aware Self-Propagation Module, Boundary-Anchored Patch Discriminator, and Style-Diversity Object Generator.
Extensive experiments are conducted on Cityscapes dataset to show the performance of the proposed method.
Experiments are also conducted on ADE20K-Room datasets to evaluate the proposed method.
The method outperforms the baselines in synthesizing objects and backgrounds.

The framework, Semantic Image Editing by Disentangling Object and Background (SIEDOB), aims to generate backgrounds and foreground objects separately to tackle limitations and achieve texture consistency
SIEDOB disassembles the edited image into background regions and instance-level objects to be synthesized separately by different generators
Generated objects are aggregated with backgrounds via a fusion network in the presented framework
Generating texture-consistent backgrounds with known context is challenging due to the spatial area backgrounds cover and their irregular shapes
A boosted generator and discriminator pair is proposed to transfer semantic-wise features from known regions to generated regions efficiently
A Boundary-Anchored Patch Discriminator is designed to enhance texture consistency by forcing the generator to pay attention to boundaries in the image
The Boundary-Anchored Patch Discriminator is designed to force the generator to pay more attention to local textures of editing fringe.
Depending on the scenario, the edited object may be requested to be inpainted.
If an object is partially visible, it undergoes a lightweight inpainting network. Otherwise, a Style-Diversity Object Generator is employed to obtain multi-modal results based on a style bank.
After generating the backgrounds and all objects, they are re-integrated into a whole to avoid sudden boundaries and dissonance between objects and surroundings.

Separate generation may lead to sudden boundaries and dissonance between objects and surroundings. To tackle this problem, a fusion network is utilized to predict the residual value for each pixel and harmonize outputs.
A new solution for semantic image editing named SIEDOB is proposed, which can handle complex scenes by decoupling the generation of backgrounds and objects.
The Boundary-Anchored Patch Discriminator and Semantic-Aware Self-Propagation Module are proposed to facilitate texture-consistent background generation.
A Style-Diversity Object Generator is proposed to generate diverse and realistic object images from masks.
Extensive experiments on two benchmarks indicate that the method can generate texture-consistent results.
Semantic image editing refers to modifying a given image by manipulating a corresponding segmentation map and keeping the context coherent.
This approach provides powerful editing capabilities, such as adding and removing objects, modifying shapes, and re-painting backgrounds.

Compared to several related tasks, including semantic image synthesis and image inpainting, semantic image editing has difficulty in generating realistic objects and texture-consistent backgrounds simultaneously.
HIM is an early exploration that only operates on a single object using a two-stage network.

SESAME introduces a new pair of generators and discriminators to improve the quality of the generated results. Moreover, SESAME adopts a more flexible workflow that can respond to various image editing requirements of users.
ASSET proposes a novel transformer-based approach modeling long-range dependencies, enabling high-resolution image editing.
SPMPGAN presents a style-preserved modulation technique and builds a progressive architecture that solves the style inconsistency problem in semantic image editing.
The preserved modulation technique and progressive architecture are mentioned as part of the proposed solution to address style inconsistency in semantic image editing.
The methods discussed consider semantic image editing as a global generation problem, where a single model is used to process all elements of the edited image equally.
The decoupled framework proposed in the work is designed to handle objects and backgrounds separately using heterogeneous generators.

node_2: real image patches or fake image patches
edge: Boundary-Anchored Patch Discriminator DBAP takes in four real image patches or fake image patches of random size.
The Boundary-Anchored Patch Discriminator DBAP is a novel discriminator introduced to enforce the generator to pay attention to local texture across the fringe of the edited area.
DBAP helps the generator to capture known and generated textures simultaneously to facilitate texture-consistent results.
The generator focuses on the edited area to ensure texture consistency in the results.
The generator randomly selects several central points anchored on the boundary of the edited region to crop patches from real or generated images.
DBAP can distinguish between true and false textures through texture consistency, as the fake patches contain both real and fake textures.
